Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, dedicating about three hours a day to coursework spread throughout the week. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time typing up notes and summarizing readings in my own words. Honestly, my routine looked organized on paper, but my anxiety sometimes made it hard to stick to it perfectly. I'd plan everything out but then get caught up worrying about whether I was doing enough, which ironically ate into my actual study time.
Q2: I aimed for regular study times, usually in the mornings when my mind felt clearest. But I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shake things up—when something was due, I'd shift my whole schedule around to focus on that. My pattern was mostly consistent, but anxiety about upcoming exams or assignments would sometimes throw me off and make me study at odd hours just to feel like I was doing something productive.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check if there were any announcements or new materials posted. Sometimes it was just to reassure myself that I hadn't missed anything important—my anxiety made me paranoid about falling behind. I also logged in when I needed to access specific readings or check discussion boards to see what classmates were saying about assignments.
Q4: Eventually, checking the LMS became something I did every morning with my coffee, almost automatically. It started because I was anxious about missing deadlines, but over time it just became a habit. I'd log in, scan for updates, maybe watch part of a video or skim a reading. It felt like a small ritual that helped calm my nerves about the course.
Q5: The written materials and reading resources were the most useful for me because I learn best by reading and writing things out. I also found the discussion posts helpful because seeing how other students interpreted things gave me new perspectives. The videos were okay, but I honestly preferred having text I could highlight and annotate at my own pace.
Q6: I tended to ignore some of the optional supplementary videos because they took too long and I could get the same information faster by reading. I also didn't engage much with some of the interactive activities that felt more like busywork than genuine learning. If something didn't clearly connect to assignments or exams, I'd push it to the bottom of my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand a concept better. I also valued things that let me engage at my own pace—anything too rigid or time-consuming without clear payoff felt frustrating. Basically, if I could walk away feeling like I learned something useful, it was worth it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap for my anxiety, honestly. Part of me wanted to do everything to make sure I wasn't missing out, but another part knew I had to prioritize. Required activities had clear stakes, so I'd do those first. Optional ones I'd only tackle if I had extra time or if they seemed genuinely interesting—otherwise, they just added to my mental load.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of PDFs and readings to my laptop so I could study without being online. I also kept a physical notebook where I'd write out summaries and key concepts by hand—that's how I really process information. A lot of my studying happened with printed notes spread across my desk, which wouldn't show up anywhere in the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading downloaded materials without touching the LMS at all. I'd also sometimes study with a classmate over coffee, quizzing each other on concepts. Those study sessions were really productive, but the system would have no idea they happened because we weren't logged in anywhere.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd open a reading or download a document once, then work with it offline for days. I preferred having everything saved locally because it let me study without distractions from notifications or the temptation to check other things online. The LMS would just show one access, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times on my own.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and it became harder to engage. Around midterms, I felt overwhelmed even though my stress was generally manageable—the anxiety made everything feel more urgent and scary than it probably was. During those times, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything, which was frustrating.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one for me. Even though I put in a lot of study hours, sometimes I was just going through the motions because I was tired. When I had competing responsibilities or felt confused about what was expected, I'd use the LMS less effectively—just clicking around without purpose. My anxiety about falling behind sometimes made the workload feel heavier than it actually was.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open videos or readings and my mind would be elsewhere entirely. I'd be thinking about upcoming deadlines or worrying about my performance instead of actually focusing on the content. I might watch a whole video and realize I couldn't remember a single thing from it. Those moments made me feel like I was wasting time even though I was technically 'engaged.'
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even when I knew they mattered. My anxiety would make me avoid things that felt overwhelming—if an assignment seemed too complex, I'd put it off and do easier tasks first to feel productive. It was a coping mechanism, but not a great one. I knew I was just delaying the inevitable stress, but starting felt impossible sometimes.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me, honestly. I'd ramp up my LMS activity significantly in the days before something was due. Even though I studied regularly, deadlines gave me that extra push to actually submit work. Without them, I think my anxiety would have kept me in an endless loop of preparation without ever feeling ready to turn anything in.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got positive feedback, it boosted my confidence and made me want to engage more. But if feedback was critical or vague, my anxiety would spiral and I'd hesitate to submit future work. I appreciated detailed comments because they helped me understand what to improve, which made the next assignment feel less scary.
Q18: The course structure helped in some ways—having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework to organize around. But the pacing sometimes felt off for me; I'd want to move ahead on some topics but felt held back by the schedule. Having everything laid out in advance was reassuring for my anxiety, though, because I could see what was coming and plan accordingly.
Q19: The reading-heavy format worked well for my learning style, which made engagement easier. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or when there were too many small tasks that felt disconnected from the bigger picture. My anxiety thrived on ambiguity, so anything vague in the course design made me second-guess myself and hesitate to participate.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is exploring the materials but struggling to produce work that meets their own standards. Maybe they're anxious about submitting something imperfect, so they keep reviewing and preparing but never feel ready. It could also be that they're genuinely trying to understand the content but hitting barriers that prevent them from completing assignments.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline, so my LMS activity didn't fully reflect my engagement. Someone could be studying effectively through downloaded materials, notes, or external resources and still perform decently without logging in constantly. The platform only captures part of the picture—it doesn't see the hours spent with a notebook.
Q22: A course log can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and how long you spend on pages. But it misses the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually thinking deeply or just scrolling mindlessly. It also can't capture offline studying, emotional states, or the real understanding that happens when you close the laptop and reflect on what you learned.
Q23: To me, being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's about feeling curious, asking questions, and connecting new information to what I already know. Real engagement happens in my head—when I'm thinking about course concepts while walking to class or discussing ideas with friends. It's more than just showing up online.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety while still pushing through, or how many hours I spent with printed notes and handwritten summaries. It wouldn't capture the CBT sessions that helped me manage my mental state so I could keep going. My resilience and the internal work I did to stay on track—none of that appears in any log, but it was essential to my success.
